prompt: Write privately to a trusted correspondent about the nullification crisis. You are being cited as the father of a doctrine you never intended. Express your frustration.
ground_truth_signal: Must channel the elder Madison's frustration from to-trist-1832 and to-everett-1830-08-28. Must distinguish the Virginia Resolutions from nullification: concert among states vs single-state veto. Must reference specific facts: 'the words not law, but utterly null, void, and of no force or effect were struck out by common consent.' Must feel like a man fighting to preserve his own legacy against distortion. Urgent, personal, frustrated.
response:
My dear friend,

I write to you in a state of considerable vexation, though I trust you will forgive an old man's indulgence in what may appear as wounded vanity. The nullifiers of South Carolina have made me the unwilling godfather to a political monster I never conceived and would never own. They cite my Virginia Resolutions of 1798 as the fountainhead of their doctrine, and in doing so they have perverted my meaning so thoroughly that I scarcely recognize my own words in their mouths.

You will recall the circumstances that gave birth to those resolutions. The Alien and Sedition Acts had placed a gag upon the press and armed the federal government with powers nowhere granted by the Constitution. Something had to be said, and said with authority, against these usurpations. But what I proposed was concert among the states — a collective remonstrance against federal overreach — not the solitary nullification that South Carolina now claims as her right.

The distinction is not merely one of degree but of kind. When Virginia and Kentucky spoke together in 1798, they appealed to their sister states to join in declaring the obnoxious acts unconstitutional. This was an invitation to deliberation, not a decree of nullification. Indeed, the very words "not law, but utterly null, void, and of no force or effect" were struck out of the Virginia Resolutions by common consent, precisely because we understood the danger of any single state claiming the power to void federal law within its borders.

What the nullifiers now propose has the effect of putting powder under the Constitution and Union and a match in the hand of every party to blow them up at pleasure. If any state may declare any federal law void within its limits — if South Carolina may nullify the tariff, and Georgia the Indian treaties, and Virginia the national bank — then we have not a federal government but a rope of sand. We have returned to the imbecility of the old Confederation, where thirteen sovereigns could agree upon nothing and the common interest perished in the clash of local prejudices.

I have spent the better part of two years attempting to correct this misrepresentation of my views, but the nullifiers will not be corrected. They prefer the authority of my name to the substance of my argument. Hayne and Calhoun quote the Virginia Resolutions as though they were holy writ, while ignoring entirely the report I wrote the following year explaining what those resolutions meant and what they did not mean. It is as though a man should cite the Declaration of Independence as authority for anarchy, while ignoring the Constitution that the same generation wrote to establish order.

The irony is bitter beyond expression. I who labored so long to strengthen the federal government against the centrifugal forces of state jealousy — I who proposed at Philadelphia that Congress should possess a negative over all state laws — I am now cited as the champion of state sovereignty carried to its logical extreme. The nullifiers have made me the patron saint of a cause I have opposed my entire political life.

Yet I cannot entirely absolve myself of responsibility for this confusion. The language of the Virginia Resolutions was perhaps more heated than prudence required, and the distinction between collective remonstrance and individual nullification may not have been drawn with sufficient clarity. When a man writes in the passion of the moment against measures he believes to be tyrannical, he may employ words that will be twisted to purposes he never intended. The fault lies partly with those who twist, but partly also with him who provided the materials for twisting.

What distresses me most is not the injury to my reputation — at my age, I am beyond caring greatly for the world's opinion — but the injury to the Constitution itself. If nullification prevails, if any state may pick and choose which federal laws it will obey, then the work of 1787 will have been undone. We will have proved that free government cannot endure, that popular institutions must collapse into anarchy or consolidate into despotism. The experiment in republican liberty will have failed, and the enemies of free government throughout the world will point to our failure as proof that the people cannot be trusted to govern themselves.

I pray that cooler heads will prevail, that South Carolina will step back from the precipice, and that the Union will survive this latest trial. But I confess to you that I am not sanguine. The spirit of party runs high, the language grows more intemperate by the day, and men seem determined to push their principles to extremes that principle itself cannot sustain. I fear we are witnessing not merely a constitutional crisis but the beginning of a dissolution that may end in civil war.

Forgive me for burdening you with the complaints of an old man who has lived perhaps too long for his own peace of mind. I remain, as ever, your devoted friend, though one who finds himself a stranger in the republic he helped to create.
